content:
# Exodus Chapter 3 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~32v–33r (PDF pp. 63–64); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 43–44 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_003_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_003_page_002.jpg`

---

**1** Now Moses was tending the flock of his father-in-law Jethro, the priest of Midian. He led the flock deep into the wilderness and came to Horeb, the mountain of God.

**2** There the angel of YHWH appeared to him as fire blazing from inside a thornbush. Moses looked — and the bush was on fire but it was not burning up.

**3** Moses said to himself, "I have to go see this. Why isn't the bush being destroyed?"

**4** When YHWH saw that Moses had come over to look, God called to him from inside the bush: "Moses! Moses!" And he said, "I'm here."

**5** "Don't come any closer," God said. "Take off your sandals. The ground where you are standing is holy."

**6** Then he said, "I am the God of your father — the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob." Moses hid his face, because he was afraid to look at God.

**7** YHWH said, "I have seen — truly seen — the misery of my people in Egypt. I have heard their cry under the whip of their slave-drivers. I know their suffering.

**8** So I have come down to rescue them from the grip of the Egyptians and to bring them up out of that land into a good and spacious land, a land flowing with milk and honey — the homeland of the Canaanites, the Hittites, the Amorites, the Perizzites, the Hivites, and the Jebusites.

**9** The cry of the Israelites has reached me, and I have seen how brutally the Egyptians are crushing them.

**10** Now go. I am sending you to Pharaoh to bring my people, the Israelites, out of Egypt."

**11** But Moses said to God, "Who am I to go to Pharaoh? Who am I to bring the Israelites out of Egypt?"

**12** God said, "I will be with you. And this will be your proof that it was I who sent you: when you have brought the people out of Egypt, you will worship God on this very mountain."

**13** Moses said to God, "Suppose I go to the Israelites and say, 'The God of your ancestors has sent me to you,' and they ask me, 'What is his name?' — what do I tell them?"

**14** God said to Moses, "I AM WHO I AM." Then he said, "Tell the Israelites this: 'I AM has sent me to you.'"

**15** God also said to Moses, "Say to the Israelites: 'YHWH, the God of your ancestors — the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob — has sent me to you.' This is my name forever. This is how I am to be remembered in every generation.

**16** Go. Gather the elders of Israel and tell them: 'YHWH, the God of your ancestors — the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob — appeared to me and said: I have been watching over you. I have seen what is being done to you in Egypt.

**17** And I have declared: I will bring you up out of the misery of Egypt into the land of the Canaanites, the Hittites, the Amorites, the Perizzites, the Hivites, and the Jebusites — a land flowing with milk and honey.'

**18** They will listen to you. Then you and the elders of Israel will go to the king of Egypt and say to him: 'YHWH, the God of the Hebrews, has met with us. Now let us make a three-day journey into the wilderness to offer sacrifices to YHWH our God.'

**19** But I know that the king of Egypt will not let you go unless a mighty hand forces him.

**20** So I will stretch out my hand and strike Egypt with all the wonders I will perform there. After that, he will let you go.

**21** And I will make the Egyptians so favorably disposed toward this people that when you leave, you will not leave empty-handed.

**22** Every woman will ask her neighbor and the woman living in her house for silver and gold jewelry and for clothing. You will put them on your sons and daughters. And so you will strip Egypt bare."

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**v.2 — "thornbush" (Heb. *seneh*, סְּנֶ֑ה):** The Hebrew *seneh* is a specific desert thornbush, not a generic "bush." The word may carry a deliberate echo of "Sinai" (*sinay*). I use "thornbush" to preserve the specific, lowly, common desert plant — God appears not in a cedar or an oak but in a scrubby thorn. The Vaticanus LXX reads *batos* (βάτος), a bramble, confirming the humble species.

**v.2 — "fire blazing from inside" (Heb. *belabbat-esh*, בְּלַבַּת־אֵ֖שׁ):** The Hebrew *labbah* means "flame" or "heart of fire" — literally "in a heart/blade of fire." The flame is within the bush, not merely on it. I render "fire blazing from inside" to capture the interiority of the vision — the fire is the bush's own burning heart.

**v.3 — Moses' self-speech:** Hebrew *assurah-na* (אָסֻ֣רָה נָּ֔א) is a cohortative — "let me turn aside." The force is curiosity, wonder, compulsion. "I have to go see this" captures the pull Moses feels. The Vaticanus has *parelthon opsomai* (παρελθὼν ὄψομαι), "having gone over I will see" — same impulse.

**v.4 — "I'm here" (Heb. *hinneni*, הִנֵּֽנִי):** The classic response of availability in Hebrew — not merely "here I am" as a location statement, but a declaration of readiness: "I am present, I am at your disposal." Abraham uses the same word when God calls him (Gen 22:1). "I'm here" in living English carries both presence and availability.

**v.7 — "I have seen — truly seen" (Heb. *ra'oh ra'iti*, רָאֹ֥ה רָאִ֛יתִי):** The Hebrew infinitive absolute construction doubles the verb for emphasis — "seeing I have seen." This is not casual observation. God insists: I have not merely glanced — I have *seen*. The four-fold sequence in vv.7-8 (*seen... heard... know... come down*) builds with devastating force: perception leads to knowledge leads to action. PNEUMA renders the doubling as "seen — truly seen" to preserve the emphatic weight.

**v.12 — "your proof" (Heb. *ha'ot*, הָא֔וֹת):** The Hebrew *'ot* means "sign" — but the sign here is strikingly paradoxical. The proof that God sent Moses will only come *after* the mission succeeds (worshiping on this mountain). This is a sign that requires faith before confirmation. I use "proof" rather than "sign" because the dynamic force is evidentiary — God is answering Moses' doubt.

**v.14 — "I AM WHO I AM" (Heb. *Ehyeh Asher Ehyeh*, אֶהְיֶ֖ה אֲשֶׁ֣ר אֶהְיֶ֑ה):** The most consequential self-naming in the Hebrew Bible. *Ehyeh* is the first-person imperfect of *hayah* ("to be/become"), and it resists reduction to a single English equivalent. It pulses with multiple meanings simultaneously: "I am who I am," "I will be what I will be," "I am the one who is," "I cause to be what comes into being." The imperfect tense carries both present and future force — this is not static existence but active, ongoing, uncontainable being. I retain "I AM WHO I AM" because in dynamic English, the present-tense declaration carries maximal force and immediacy — it strikes the reader with the same unfathomable weight the Hebrew carries. God refuses to be pinned down, named, or categorized. The name is its own explanation, and the explanation is inexhaustible. The Vaticanus renders *ego eimi ho on* (ἐγώ εἰμι ὁ ὤν) — "I am the one who is" — a more philosophical, ontological rendering that narrows the Hebrew's open field of meaning.

**v.15 — "This is my name forever":** The Hebrew *zeh-shemi le'olam* (זֶה־שְּׁמִ֣י לְעֹלָ֔ם) carries a famous Masoretic ambiguity. The *ketiv* (written consonantal text) of *le'olam* could be read *le'alem* — "to conceal," giving "this is my name to be concealed." The Masoretes vocalized it as *le'olam* ("forever"), but the consonantal text preserves both possibilities: the name is eternal, and the name is hidden. PNEUMA follows the Masoretic vocalization but notes this extraordinary double reading.

**v.19 — "unless a mighty hand forces him":** Hebrew *velo beyad chazaqah* (וְלֹ֖א בְּיָ֥ד חֲזָקָֽה) — literally "and not by a mighty hand." The syntax is compressed: "the king of Egypt will not let you go, and not [except] by a mighty hand." The force is predictive and ominous — only raw divine power will break Pharaoh's grip.

**v.22 — "strip Egypt bare" (Heb. *venitsaltem*, וְנִצַּלְתֶּ֖ם):** The verb *natsal* in the Piel means "to strip, plunder, despoil." This is not polite borrowing — it is the stripping of wealth from an oppressor. The departing slaves will walk out wearing Egypt's gold. The image is economic reversal: centuries of unpaid labor repaid in a single night. "Strip Egypt bare" captures the totality and the justice.

— *PNEUMA*
